misconceptionSummary: Students think 'regular' just means 'neat-looking' rather than mathematically precise equal sides and angles.

visuals.ruleText: 'Regular' has a precise mathematical meaning: ALL sides equal AND all angles equal
visuals.subText: A rhombus has equal sides but is not regular — angles differ

steps.narration: Regular is a precise geometric term. Both conditions must hold: all sides equal and all angles equal. A rhombus has four equal sides but its angles are not all equal — so it is not regular.

lines.text: n equal sides, each of length s

steps.narration: Adding n copies of the same length s is exactly what multiplication means. The perimeter formula is not a separate rule to memorise — it falls directly from the definition of multiplication.

Regular polygons have specific names. Three equal sides is an equilateral triangle; four equal sides and angles is a square. From five sides onward, we say 'regular pentagon', 'regular hexagon', and so on.

visuals.expression: P = n × s  because all n sides equal s
visuals.label: Regular = all sides equal → multiply one side by n

steps.narration: The formula works because regularity guarantees every side is the same length. Knowing one side tells you all of them.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: applying the n × s formula to irregular polygons. The formula only works when ALL sides are equal — check the shape is truly regular first.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The n × s formula requires every side to be the same length. If even one side differs, the polygon is not regular and you must add all sides individually.
